file_analyses.file_purpose: Page object for the Update Plan page of the Instant Ink application. Contains all UI element locators for plan selection, plan details, upgrade/downgrade modals, pause/resume functionality, and plan management actions. Provides methods to dynamically retrieve plan elements by page count or position, and properties for accessing specific UI elements with special handling like the cancel instant ink link and free trial info card.

classes.class_purpose: Encapsulates all UI elements and user interactions for the Update Plan page where users can view their current plan details, change to different page count plans (10, 25, 50, 100, 300, 500, 700, 1500 pages), upgrade or downgrade plans, pause or resume plans, cancel Instant Ink subscription, manage paper add-ons, update shipping and billing information, and redeem prepaid codes. Contains extensive locator definitions organized by functional areas including plan details card, plan selection cards, downgrade/upgrade modals, pause plan controls, header links, and page elements. Extends BasePageObject for common page interaction patterns.

parameters.name: page
parameters.description: Playwright Page instance representing the browser page

functions.what_it_does: Initializes the UpdatePlanPage by calling the parent BasePageObject constructor with super().__init__(page) to set up the page instance. Then defines all 48 UI element locators as attributes on the self.elements object. The locators are organized into logical groups: Plan Details card elements (plan_details_card, replace_printer_link, cancel_instant_ink, cancel_paper_add_on, contact_support_link, change_shipping_link, change_billing_info_link, prepaid_code_link, free_trial_plan_details, paper_free_months, pause_plan_info, resume_plan_link, special_offers_balance, plan_information), Plan Cards for different page counts (plan_10 through plan_1500 covering positions 0-7), Downgrade Plan Modal elements (downgrade_plan_confirmation, cancel_change_plan_button, cancel_change_plan_link, undo_plan_change_cancel_button, undo_plan_change_confirmation, change_plan_button, plan_downgrade_close_modal_button), Upgrade Plan Modal elements (upgrade_plan_confirmation, upgrade_next_month, upgrade_current_month), Pause Plan elements (plan_100_resume_tag, plan_100_resume_button), Header elements (header_cancel_link, header_remove_paper_link), and Page elements (page_title, plan_card_container, free_trial_info_card, disclaimer_card, print_history_link, terms_and_service_link, pending_change_plan, change_plan_button_10 through change_plan_button_1500, dashboard_plan_selector_paper_card_button). All locators use data-testid attributes for reliable element identification, with some using CSS class selectors or composite selectors for specific targeting.

parameters.name: pages
parameters.description: The page count for the plan (10, 25, 50, 100, 300, 500, 700, or 1500)

functions.what_it_does: Dynamically retrieves the change plan button selector for a specific page count plan. Constructs an attribute name by concatenating 'change_plan_button_' with the pages parameter (e.g., 'change_plan_button_100' for pages=100). Uses hasattr() to check if the UpdatePlanPage instance has an attribute with that name. If the attribute exists, uses getattr() to retrieve and return the selector value from self.elements. If the attribute does not exist (meaning the page count is not one of the supported plans), raises a ValueError with a descriptive message indicating that the plan with the specified page count was not found. This method provides a programmatic way to access plan buttons without hardcoding the specific page count in test code.

parameters.name: plan_number
parameters.description: The zero-based position index of the plan card (0-7)

functions.what_it_does: Retrieves a Playwright Locator object for a plan card based on its position index in the plan selector. Constructs a data-testid selector string by formatting 'plans-selector-plan-card-container-{plan_number}' with the provided plan_number parameter. Calls self.page.locator() with the constructed selector string to create and return a Playwright Locator object that can be used for interactions like clicking, checking visibility, or extracting text. This method allows tests to interact with plan cards by their position rather than by page count, which is useful when the exact page count mapping is not known or when iterating through all available plans.

parameters.name: pages
parameters.description: The page count for the plan (10, 25, 50, 100, 300, 500, 700, or 1500)

functions.what_it_does: Dynamically retrieves the plan card selector for a specific page count plan. Constructs an attribute name by concatenating 'plan_' with the pages parameter (e.g., 'plan_100' for pages=100). Uses hasattr() to check if the UpdatePlanPage instance has an attribute with that name on self.elements. If the attribute exists, uses getattr() to retrieve and return the selector value from self.elements. If the attribute does not exist (meaning the page count is not one of the supported plans), raises a ValueError with a descriptive message indicating that the plan with the specified page count was not found. This method provides a programmatic way to access plan card selectors without hardcoding the specific page count in test code, enabling data-driven test approaches.

Property method that returns a Playwright Locator object for the cancel instant ink link with special handling. Accesses the cancel_instant_ink selector from self.elements.cancel_instant_ink, passes it to self.page.locator() to create a Locator object, and then calls .last on the result to get the last matching element. This is necessary because there may be multiple cancel links on the page (one in the plan details card and one in the header), and this property ensures the one in the plan details card is selected by taking the last occurrence. Returns the Locator object that can be used for clicking or checking visibility. The @property decorator allows this method to be accessed like an attribute without parentheses.

Property method that returns a Playwright Locator object for the free trial info card with special handling. Accesses the free_trial_info_card selector from self.elements.free_trial_info_card, passes it to self.page.locator() to create a Locator object, and then calls .first on the result to get the first matching element. This is necessary because the class selector may match multiple elements on the page, and this property ensures the correct free trial info card is selected by taking the first occurrence. Returns the Locator object that can be used for extracting text, checking visibility, or other interactions. The @property decorator allows this method to be accessed like an attribute without parentheses.

file_analyses.analysis_notes: This page object follows a comprehensive locator organization pattern with 48 distinct UI element locators covering all aspects of the Update Plan page. The class uses the self.elements pattern from BasePageObject to store locators as attributes rather than direct instance attributes. It provides three utility methods for dynamic plan selection: get_select_plan_button() for retrieving change plan buttons by page count, get_plan_by_position() for retrieving plan cards by position index, and get_plan_by_pages() for retrieving plan card selectors by page count. Two property methods (cancel_instant_ink and free_trial_info_card) provide special handling for elements that may have multiple matches on the page, using .last and .first respectively. The locators extensively use data-testid attributes which are the most reliable selector strategy. Some locators use attribute starts-with selectors (^=) for flexibility, and a few use CSS class selectors with starts-with matching. The page supports all eight plan tiers (10, 25, 50, 100, 300, 500, 700, 1500 pages) with dedicated locators for each. The class handles complex workflows including plan upgrades with immediate or next-month options, plan downgrades with confirmation modals, plan pause and resume functionality, and plan cancellation. This is a mature page object with extensive coverage of a complex UI.

file_analyses.file_purpose: Test data payload file for RTPDS (Real-Time Printer Data Service) with IIC (Instant Ink Cartridge) configuration. Contains a complete JSON payload structure for simulating printer supply status reports with four separate ink cartridges (Yellow, Magenta, Cyan, Black). Includes device information, firmware version, remote attestation data, supply status for each color cartridge with detailed ink metadata, and service issuer information. Uses template variables ({{variable_name}}) for dynamic value substitution during test execution.
file_analyses.usage_notes: This payload is used for testing RTPDS printer data submission scenarios with four separate ink cartridges (IIC configuration). Template variables must be replaced with actual values before sending the payload. The {{uuid}} variable is used with color suffixes (-Y, -M, -C, -K) to create unique consumable IDs for each cartridge. All cartridges are configured as starter cartridges at 100% level with genuine HP assessment and requesting supply fulfillment. The payload includes remote attestation data with signature and session data for security validation. The newCred_override field is set to 'True' as a string. Device violations array is empty indicating no issues. Supply insertion count and various sequence numbers are parameterized for test flexibility.

file_analyses.file_purpose: Test data payload file for RTPDS (Real-Time Printer Data Service) with IPH (Instant Ink Print Head) configuration. Contains a complete JSON payload structure for simulating printer supply status reports with two combined ink supplies (CMY combined color cartridge and K black cartridge). Includes device information, firmware version, remote attestation data, supply status for each cartridge with detailed ink metadata, and service issuer information. Uses template variables ({{variable_name}}) for dynamic value substitution during test execution. This represents a different printer cartridge configuration than IIC where colors are combined rather than separate.

json_structure.differences_from_iic: IPH configuration uses only 2 supply entries (CMY combined and K) instead of 4 separate color cartridges (Y, M, C, K). The consumableUniqueId uses '-supply-cmy' and '-supply-k' suffixes instead of single letter color codes. This represents printers with combined color cartridges rather than individual color cartridges.

file_analyses.usage_notes: This payload is used for testing RTPDS printer data submission scenarios with combined color cartridge configuration (IPH). Template variables must be replaced with actual values before sending the payload. The {{uuid}} variable is used with supply type suffixes (-supply-cmy, -supply-k) to create unique consumable IDs. Both cartridges are configured as starter cartridges at 100% level with genuine HP assessment and requesting supply fulfillment. The payload structure is identical to IIC except for the supply configuration. Use this payload for testing printers that use combined CMY cartridges rather than separate color cartridges.

file_analyses.file_purpose: Empty Python package initializer file that marks the test_flows_common directory as a Python package, allowing modules within this directory to be imported using Python's import system. This is a standard Python convention for package structure.
file_analyses.analysis_notes: This is an empty __init__.py file with zero bytes. It serves only to make the test_flows_common directory importable as a Python package. No code analysis is needed as the file contains no code. This is a standard Python packaging practice.

file_analyses.file_purpose: Comprehensive test data file containing shipping address information for 32 countries/regions used in Instant Ink enrollment and address management testing. Each country has 3 address entries: two valid addresses with real postal codes and one invalid address with a fake postal code for negative testing. Addresses include all required fields for international shipping including street addresses, cities, states/provinces, postal codes, phone numbers, and country codes. This data supports testing of address validation, shipping address entry, address change functionality, and international coverage across all supported Instant Ink markets.

json_structure.root_structure: Object with country names as keys, each containing an array of 3 address objects
json_structure.address_pattern_per_country: First address: Valid address with real postal code. Second address: Alternative valid address with real postal code. Third address: Valid address with invalid/fake postal code for negative testing.

special_cases.Hong Kong: Has fullState in both English (fullState_en-HK) and Chinese (fullState_zh-HK). zipCode is empty string as Hong Kong does not use postal codes.
special_cases.Singapore: City and state fields are empty strings as Singapore is a city-state.
special_cases.Puerto Rico: Uses US-style addressing with 5-digit ZIP codes and country code PR.

file_analyses.usage_notes: This data file is used for testing address entry and validation across all supported Instant Ink countries. Each country has 3 addresses: 2 valid for positive testing and 1 with invalid postal code for negative testing. Tests can iterate through countries and addresses to verify international address handling. The ignoreValidationErrors flag is set to true for all addresses, suggesting these are used in contexts where validation may be bypassed. Phone numbers include country codes. State codes use both abbreviated (state) and full name (fullState) formats. Some countries have empty state fields where not applicable. The third address in each country array typically has a fake postal code (like '12345', '1234', 'ABC1234') for testing validation error handling.
data_access_examples: address_data['United States'][0] - First valid US address
address_data['Germany'][2] - Invalid German address with fake postal code
address_data['Hong Kong'][0] - Valid Hong Kong address with Chinese state name
Loop through all countries: for country, addresses in address_data.items()
Get all valid addresses: [addr for addrs in address_data.values() for addr in addrs[:2]]

file_analyses.commit_message: [Aava] Batch 16 analysis report | 5 files analyzed | 1 classes | 6 methods | 48 locators
